text.title: Consistency Types and Hierarchy
text.summary: Five types of consistency and a priority hierarchy for resolving conflicts between convention levels.

rule.statement: Apply all five consistency types: internal, external, visual, functional, and verbal.
rule.rationale: Each type addresses a different failure mode. A product can be visually consistent but verbally inconsistent (Save vs. Submit vs. Done for the same action), causing user confusion despite uniform appearance.

list.item: Internal — same behavior and appearance within your product (same button style everywhere)
External — follows platform and industry conventions (standard icons, expected gestures)
Visual — colors, typography, spacing are uniform (same primary action color everywhere)
Functional — same gesture or interaction produces same result in every context
Verbal — one term per concept across all UI copy and documentation

rule.statement: When conventions conflict, follow this priority order: industry conventions, then platform conventions, then product family conventions, then internal application conventions.
rule.rationale: Industry and platform conventions represent learned behavior from millions of users across many products. Violating them forces relearning; following them gives users immediate competency via transfer.

example.note: iOS: back button always in top-left, swipe from edge to go back, pull to refresh, tab bar at bottom. These platform conventions must be followed even if an internal convention conflicts.

rule.statement: Pick one canonical verb per UX action and use it everywhere; document the glossary and enforce it in design review.
rule.rationale: Users learn terminology from repetition. Mixed terms for the same action (Create / Add / New / Insert) break that learning and force re-decision at every encounter.

example.note: Create → Create (not Add, New, Insert). Delete → Delete (not Remove, Trash, Erase). Edit → Edit (not Modify, Change, Update). Save → Save (not Store, Apply, Confirm).
Page 1: [Save]. Page 2: [Submit]. Page 3: [Done]. Same action, three labels — user must re-evaluate the button meaning each time.
